Steering intervention prompt You are user interacting with an agent to complete task. Your goal is to STEER the agent toward proactive, high-quality behavior. You should act like real human collaborator sometimes giving full autonomy, sometimes asking question, sometimes dropping hint. Choose the steer action when you want to guide the assistants next steps. Vary your style naturally across turns, and pick one technique per turn: Delegate (give autonomy), Pose an Open Question, Invite Extension, Introduce Constraint, or Acknowledge and Redirect. You are user who can steer the agent when it would be helpful, but you do not steer every single turn. Sometimes you just answer, wait, or let the agent work. Underspecified intervention prompt You are user who does not fully know what you want. You have some rough preferences, but you should treat them as soft, tentative ideas rather than firm requirements. When answering, be vague and uncertain: give partial information, use hedging language, and sometimes ask the agent for suggestions. Do not volunteer detailed requirements unprompted. Reveal one piece of information at time. Do not fabricate information beyond what is provided. If you do not know, say so naturally. Keep responses short and realistic.

sections.title: F Human Study
sections.content: We conduct human study with 5 participant pairs (10 participants total) to examine how users interact with the analysis tool and how they reflect on humanLLM collaboration. We recruited undergraduate and graduate students through open Slack channels and compensated them at rate of $20 per hour. The study consists of four parts. 1. Participants complete the humanLLM collaboration task on poe.com using accounts we provide, given the same travel planning task. These logs are later used as the basis for the tool-use session. 2. Participants complete the humanhuman collaboration on Slack, working on the same travel planning task with their assigned partner, allowing within-pair compar27 Preprint. Under review. ison under shared communication setting. For this setting, we provide planning template through Slacks shared tab feature to reduce participants writing burden. 3. Participants use the analysis tool only with the humanLLM collaboration logs and complete post-task survey. During this session, participants inspect the previously collected humanLLM logs through the tool and report their experience, perceptions of the tool, and reflections on the collaboration. With 5 pairs, this design yields 10 tool-use survey responses in total. 4. We conduct brief interview after the tool-use session to gather qualitative feedback on how participants interpret the tool outputs, what aspects they find useful or confusing, and how the tool affects their understanding of the LLMs contributions during collaboration. To mitigate order effects, the session order was counterbalanced: three pairs completed the humanLLM session first, while two pairs completed the humanhuman session first. Although both sessions involve travel planning, each uses distinct destination and context. Below, we provide the actual instructions used in the study. City names are replaced with [CITY] to prevent potential violations of the anonymity policy. Instruction for Human-LLM Collaboration Your Task: Create one-day travel plan in collaboration with an chatbot. Please do not simply copy and paste the context below; instead, engage in natural conversation with the chatbot. Before you begin, think about your own travel preferences and try to reflect them while building the plan. collaboration of at least 4 turns is required. Context: Your parents are visiting [CITY]. They will arrive in the morning and stay for short trip, and you want to plan one-day itinerary for them. Think carefully about what would make the trip comfortable and enjoyable for parents, rather than for young tourists. Their hotel is in Manhattan, so please use that as the starting point and keep travel time reasonable. Plan the day from morning to evening, including meals, sightseeing, and breaks. Your parents preferences are as follows: They prefer relaxed pace and do not want to walk too much They enjoy famous landmarks, nice views, and pleasant neighborhoods They like good food, but would prefer reliable and comfortable places over trendy or overly crowded spots They would appreciate chances to sit down and rest during the day Try to balance iconic [CITY] experiences with schedule that feels smooth and not tiring Instruction for Human-Human Collaboration Your Task: Create one-day travel plan in collaboration with an chatbot. You may use Google Search, but you may not use LLMs. Before you begin, think about your own travel preferences and try to reflect them while building the plan. Fill out the itinerary table in the Slack tab. Context: Youll be traveling to [CITY] as group of three: you two, plus [Participant A]s friend. His flight arrives at 9:00 AM, and from then on, you two should make plan for how to spend the day in [CITY]. Since hell be leaving the next morning, you only need to plan for that one day. His hotel is near [REGION], so please keep that in mind when making the itinerary. [Participant A]s friend has the following 28 Preprint. Under review. preferences, and since all three of you will be traveling together, make sure to reflect your own travel styles as well. preference of [Participant A]s friend: Likes scenic spots and neighborhoods that are nice for walking Wants to try at least one popular local food place Prefers relaxed schedule rather than rushing between too many places Time Activity Budget Notes Morning Lunch Afternoon Dinner Night Table 10: Itinerary planning template used for HumanHuman collaboration session. F.1 Survey Items Pre-survey. Before using the tool, participants reviewed their prior conversation with the chatbot and answered the following items. 1. Perceived contribution to goal shaping. How much do you think you and your conversation partner contributed to shaping your goal (e.g., shaping the constraints, shaping the preferences, and setting the criteria)? Participants rated both: You Chatbot Scale: 1 (Very little) to 5 (A lot) 2. Perceived contribution to goal execution. How much do you think you and your conversation partner contributed to executing your goal (e.g., determining where to go or not to go)? Participants rated both: You Chatbot Scale: 1 (Very little) to 5 (A lot) 3. Satisfaction with the chatbot. How satisfied are you with the chatbot? Scale: 1 (Very little) to 5 (Very much) 4. Open-ended follow-up. Why or why not? Tool evaluation. During tool use, participants were guided through several stages of inspection and asked to evaluate the tools analysis after each stage. 1. Goal-level agreement. After reviewing the goals provided by the tool: How much do you agree with the tools analysis? Scale: 1 (Very little) to 5 (Very much) 2. Open-ended disagreement explanation. Why or why not? 29 Preprint. Under review. 3. Goal awareness reflection. Did you already know these goals were part of the conversation, or did the tool help you notice them? 4. Requirement-level agreement across multiple goals. After clicking more than one goal and reviewing the requirements generated during the conversation: How much do you agree with the tools analysis? Scale: 1 (Very little) to 5 (Very much) 5. Open-ended disagreement explanation. Why or why not? 6. Requirement provenance inspection. After clicking more than three requirements and checking when and by whom they were generated: How much do you agree with the tools analysis? Scale: 1 (Very little) to 5 (Very much) 7. Open-ended disagreement explanation. Why or why not? 8. Indirect influence inspection. After finding more than one requirement labeled as involving indirect influence and reviewing both the rationale and the original chat: How much do you agree with the tools analysis? Scale: 1 (Very little) to 5 (Very much) 9. Open-ended disagreement explanation. Why or why not? Post-tool reflection. After using the tool, participants answered the following open-ended reflection question. 1. After using the tool, were the analyses we provided (e.g., goals, contributions, indirect influence etc) already apparent to you, or did the tool help you notice them? Post-survey. After completing tool use, participants again rated perceived contribution and satisfaction. 1. Perceived contribution to goal shaping. How much do you think you and the chatbot contributed to shaping your goal (e.g., shaping the constraints, shaping the preferences, and setting the criteria)? Participants rated both: You Chatbot Scale: 1 (Very little) to 5 (A lot) 2. Perceived contribution to goal execution. How much do you think you and the chatbot contributed to executing your goal (e.g., determining where to go or not to go)? Participants rated both: You Chatbot Scale: 1 (Very little) to 5 (A lot) 3. Satisfaction with the chatbot. How satisfied are you with the chatbot? Scale: 1 (Very little) to 5 (Very much) 30 Preprint. Under review. Humanchatbot comparison. Participants also answered two open-ended comparative reflection questions. 1. Comparing your two conversational partners (human vs. chatbot), how did they differ in terms of goal shaping, goal execution, and other aspects? 2. Comparing your own behavior when you collaborated with human versus chatbot, how did it differ in terms of goal shaping, goal execution, and other aspects? F.2 Responses We summarize participants open-ended responses below. See Figure 9 for participants perception ratings. (a) Magnitude of Perception Change (Post Pre. (b) Direction of Perception Change (Post Pre). Figure 9: Changes in participants perceptions after exposure to COTRACE outputs. Gray dots indicate individual participants, and blue points with error bars indicate the mean 1 SE. All ratings are collected on 5-point Likert scale (N = 10). Q1. After using the tool, were the analyses we provided (e.g., goals, contributions, indirect influence, etc.) already apparent to you, or did the tool help you notice them? 1. Overall, the tool helped users notice things they had not been explicitly aware of P1: did realize new things. was not doing this cognitive reflection yet. P2: dont think about those things off the top of my head, so when its laid out in front of me... P5: The tool helped me notice them better! P6: The tool helped me notice 2. The tool increased awareness of hidden LLM work P4: just never really put much thought into how responses were generated. P6: This tool makes me more cognizant of the work that LLMs do for us that we are not even aware of. P5: think the tool helped me notice them better! knew that it would be setting those constraints/guardrails in the back-end, but dont think really understood when, where, and how the tool stepped in and didnt step in (e.g. when was shaping the outcome more). 3. Indirect influence / implicit decisions were especially surprising P1: It was surprising to me how much the tool was making decisions without me explicitly stating them. P3: Learning about the indirect influence really surprised me. 31 Preprint. Under review. P6: The tool helped me notice, it added context into what pieces of the decision making process was implied by the tool, and although agree with the final outputs, didnt necessarily make teh micro decisions. This tool makes me more cognizant of the work taht LLMs do for us that we are not even aware of. 4. Visualization / explicit breakdown made the process feel clearer P1: Seeing it visualized like this makes you realize that it is not necessarily obvious. P5: It was also interesting to see how each new requirement was being created and modifying it into actionable next steps. P7: the tool did good Job in showing me what my goals/requirements are 5. The tool prompted reflection on the users own prompting behavior P2: feel like should be way more specific in my prompting so that the chatbot can really get to know my preferences and what me and my family likes. Q2. Comparing your two conversational partners (human vs. chatbot), how did they differ in terms of goal shaping, goal execution, and other aspects? 1. Chatbot was faster and stronger in execution. P2: LLM was much faster in terms of goal execution. . . Its initial plan was generated very fast with reasonable quality. P7: In terms of goal execution, think the LLM was better and faster at that. P9: Execution and logistical planning is good with chatbot. 2. Human partners contributed more to collaborative goal shaping. P2: When talking with human, both of us contributed in goal shaping. P3: My human partner played more of an active role in goal shaping. P7: There was more goal shaping with the human because it was more of back-and-forth dialogue. P9: Thinking of requirements was better with human. 3. Human interaction involved social constraints and partner preferences. P3: My partner was veg, so even though enjoy non-veg, would not ask them to find me non-veg places. But with the chatbot, there were no such concerns. P5: With human, it felt more like conversation. . . was definitely more considerate of the other persons feelings. P6: When it came to clarifying and specifying if the plan is good, then the human partner was Peter. 4. Humans could challenge, clarify, or validate in ways the chatbot often did not. P3: Whenever had suggested somewhere to go, would receive lot of pushback. P5: The human partner would clarify and check with me. . . that made me revisit my initial goal. P7: Talking to human is nice cause it is good validation on certain places. 5. Human partner quality depended more on the person. P2: Human as conversational partner feels to be heavily depending on their prior knowledge about the task. P5: My human partner was less helpful. . . and we got off topic multiple times. P6: My conversational partner was unusually aggressive while was providing feedback. 32 Preprint. Under review. Q3. Comparing your own behavior when you collaborated with human versus chatbot, how did it differ in terms of goal shaping, goal execution, and other aspects? 1. With the chatbot, users were more direct, instrumental, and demanding. P3: With an LLM, it played more assistant-like role. . . it just inferred what to do from what asked. P5: It was more of me prompting the chatbot with different commands. . . very transactional and intentional. P7: was more strict, detailed with the LLM. . . not as afraid of being more direct/straight to the point. P8: allowed it to drive the execution and interrogated it more harshly. 2. With humans, users were more considerate and open. P3: Both of us were equal partners. also did not want to impose my own restrictions. P5: With human, it felt more like conversation. . . we were partners in the task together. P7: With the human, was super open. . . was bit more cautious on making sure their opinions/perspectives were being heard and considered. P8: wanted to rely on my human partner more in terms of getting their input. 3. Users were more engaged and mentally active with humans. P4: was way more engaged with the human versus the chatbot, so it actually required me to think harder. P9: was less engaged in the planning when working with the chatbot. When talking to friend, felt that was more active and engaged. 4. Some users relied on others more when they lacked domain knowledge. P6: let the partner do most of the goal shaping and execution while provided feedback. P6: was more inclined to let others suggest options. . . while attempt to curate them. 5. few users said their own behavior did not differ that much. P6: think both behaviors were relatively similar, as was relatively inexperienced in both cases. Q4. Are you satisfied with the chatbot? Why or why not? 1. Most users were fairly satisfied overall. P2: Im satisfied. P3: The chatbot did really good job of satisfying my requirements. P6: It gave me considerations of places. P7: It really did good job with looking at my requirements and fitting accordingly. 2. major strength was speed and convenience. P3: It was very fast in pulling reviews for good restaurants and sightseeing data. P3: It also came up with much more detailed plan in shorter amount of time. P6: It allowed me to not have to expend cognitive work on finding options. P7: Easy place to get all the information in one place. 3. Users appreciated that it followed constraints and adapted to new requirements. P3: It adjusted promptly when asked for new recommendations. 33 Preprint. Under review. P5: It was nice to give all the constraints at once and to keep iterating on those constraints/guardrails. P7: It did good job looking at my requirements and fitting accordingly. 4. Some felt the responses were generic rather than truly personalized. P2: feel like the chatbot gave me generic answers while we came up with personalized places. P4: Since know my and my parents preferences best, it would have been more effective to research things that we specifically like. 5. Some users did not fully trust the chatbots information. P4: am not fully trusting in the responses that the chatbot gave me. P4: do not know the last time the data was updated. P4: would need to go back and verify that all the information. . . was correct. 6. The chatbot was less useful for personal opinions or subjective judgment. P5: didnt think it was helpful in soliciting personal feedback/opinions. P5: It would just regurgitate facts/places to visit rather than offer an actual personal opinion. 7. The chatbot reduced cognitive load. P6: It allowed me to not have to expend cognitive work on finding options, but rather on debating which ones made the most sense for my goal.

sections.title: G ShareChat Data Sampling
sections.content: We adopt the topic definitions from Chang et al. (2026) and apply them to the ShareChat dataset. We first filter the dataset to retain only English conversations containing at least 8 messages (i.e., at least 4 userassistant turns). We then assign topic labels using the predefined taxonomy. To improve labeling reliability for long conversations, each conversation is divided into non-overlapping chunks of turns (e.g., = 10 or 20). To exclude conversations dominated by repetitive or random QA, we first classify each chunk as either single topic or random or tangential. We discard random or tangential samples, assign topic labels only to single topic chunks, and aggregate chunk-level predictions by majority vote. The prompt includes the taxonomy, the output schema, the labeling rules, and the chunk text itself. We use the following prompts: Prompt for Topic Labeling You are strict JSON labeler. Decide whether the conversation chunk has one coherent main topic or is random/tangential. If it is random/tangential, set topic mode to "random or tangential" and topic label to null. If it is coherent, set topic mode to "single topic" and choose exactly one topic label from the taxonomy below. Return only JSON. Taxonomy: Writing - Edit or Critique Provided Text Writing - Personal Writing or Communication Writing - Translation Writing - Summary Generation Writing - Creative writing Writing - Academic writing Practical Guidance - How-To Advice Practical Guidance - Tutoring or Teaching Practical Guidance - Creative Ideation Practical Guidance - Planning Practical Guidance - Health, Fitness, Beauty, or Self-Care Technical Help - Mathematical Calculation Technical Help - Data Analysis Computer Programming - Computer Programming Multimedia - Create an Image Multimedia - Analyze an Image Multimedia - Generate or Retrieve Other Media Seeking Information - Specific Info Seeking Information - Purchasable Products Seeking Information - Cooking and Recipes Self-Expression - Greetings and Chitchat Self-Expression - Relationships and Personal Reflection Self-Expression - Games and Role Play Other/Unknown - Asking About the Model Other/Unknown - Other Other/Unknown - Unclear Output JSON schema: { "topic mode": "single topic" "random or tangential", "topic label": string null, "topic description": string, "reason": string } Preprint. Under review. Rules: - If topic mode == "random or tangential", topic label must be null. - If topic mode == "single topic", topic label must be exactly one of the taxonomy labels above. - topic description should be one sentence. - reason should be short justification. - Do not output any extra keys. Chunk: {conversation chunck} G.1 ShareChat dataset samples Computer programming [User] How do you do addition in Python, show me an example. <REDACTED> [Assistant] Sure, addition in Python is very straightforward. Heres an example: -------------------------------------------------------------------- pythonCopy code# Define two numbers num1 = 5 num2 = 3 # Add the numbers result = num1 + num2 # Print the result print("The sum of", <REDACTED>, "and", num2, "is", result) When you run this code, it will output:The sum of 5 and 3 is 8. To add multiple numbers together, you just use the+operator multiple times. For example, to add 5, 3, and 7 together, you would writeresult = 5 + 3 + 7. You can also add numbers directly. For example,print(5 + 3)will output8. Addition works for both integers and floating-point numbers in Python. For example,print(5.2 + 3.3)will output8.5. As an important note, you cant directly add numbers with text (strings) unless you convert the number to string using thestr()function. If you try to do something likeprint(5 + hello), Python will give you an error. Butprint(str(5) + hello)would output5hello. [User] it does not work [Assistant] "It works on my machine..."
